notes.anchor: the kalends of January had been set as your deadline
notes.note: The Kalends of January (1 January) was the standard administrative date for new arrangements to take effect; the implication is that Caesar's government had set 1 January 45 BC as the date by which Caecina must leave Sicily, giving his exile a hard bureaucratic boundary.

notes.anchor: what Balbus and Oppius transacted in Caesar's absence is usually held by him to be ratified
notes.note: L. Cornelius Balbus and C. Oppius were Caesar's two chief agents in Rome, managing his affairs and acting as informal gatekeepers to his clemency petitions during his campaigns; Cicero's remark confirms that their word was in practice equivalent to Caesar's own.

notes.anchor: sit tight in Sicily, or set out to what remains of your business affairs in Asia
notes.note: Caecina, exiled, was managing his financial interests partly from Sicily and had business in the province of Asia; the question whether to stay near Italy (and thus Caesar's returning attention) or retreat to the east was a genuine dilemma for exiled Pompeians calculating the timing of their appeal for pardon.

notes.anchor: To Titus Furfanius Postumus, a familiar friend of mine
notes.note: T. Furfanius Postumus was proconsul of Sicily in 46-45 BC and thus Caecina's de facto host and administrator during the exile; as a Caesarian who was also a personal friend of Cicero's, he was well placed to make Caecina's stay in Sicily comfortable and politically safe.

notes.anchor: for they were all at Mutina
notes.note: Mutina (modern Modena) in Cisalpine Gaul was a Roman colony and administrative centre; Furfanius and his legates had apparently been stationed there before taking up the Sicilian command, perhaps in connection with Caesar's reorganisation of northern Italy.
